items.title: Getting Started with Docker: A Beginner’s Guide
items.author: Sachin Lakshan
items.content:
Whether you’re a developer, DevOps engineer, or simply exploring modern software deployment practices, Docker is a tool you’ll hear about a lot. It has revolutionized how we package, ship, and run applications. In this post, we’ll break down the basics of Docker, explain why containers are important, compare them with virtual machines, explore key Docker tools, and walk through Dockerizing a simple Node.js app.

Docker is a platform that helps you build, run, and ship applications more easily. It does this by putting everything your app needs — like code, dependencies, and configurations — into something called a .

Think of it like shipping containers for software: standardized, efficient, and easily movable.

So, why bother with containers? Here’s the magic behind them:

  • Consistency Across Environments: We all want our applications to behave identically in development and production environments. Containers ensure this, guaranteeing that what you test is exactly what gets deployed.
  • : Imagine a team where every member has the exact same development environment. No more “it works on my machine, but not yours!” scenarios. Docker makes it easy to share a common development environment, streamlining onboarding for new employees and collaboration among colleagues.
  • : Switching between projects often leads to clashing tools or version conflicts. With containers, the tools and dependencies used in Project A won’t interfere with those in Project B. Say goodbye to endless cycles of uninstalling and reinstalling dependencies and runtimes!

🆚 3. Virtual Machines vs Docker Containers

You might be thinking, “Isn’t Container just like a Virtual Machine?” Not quite! While both Virtual Machines (VMs) and Docker Containers offer isolation, they do so in fundamentally different ways.

As you can see, Docker containers offer a much more lightweight and efficient approach for application deployment compared to VMs.

🛠️ 4. Docker Tools & Building Blocks

To get started with Docker, you’ll encounter a few key components:

  • This is the core of Docker, running the Docker daemon and providing the command-line interface (CLI) you’ll use to interact with Docker. Often, you’ll install this via , which bundles everything you need.
  • Think of this as a vast public registry for Docker images. You can find pre-built images for almost anything, or you can push your own custom images here.
  • For multi-container applications, Docker Compose simplifies the process of defining and running them. We’ll stick to single containers for this introductory post, but it’s good to know it exists!

Here’s an overview of the , which generally follows these key stages:

  1. a Dockerfile describing your app and environment.
  2. a Docker image from it.
  3. a container from that image.
  4. your image via Docker Hub.
  5. anywhere with consistent behavior.

🧪 6. Getting Our Hands Dirty: Dockerizing a Simple Node.js App

Enough theory! Let’s get practical. We’ll Dockerize a very basic Node.js application.

  • Node.js and npm installed (for local testing).
  • on your machine (it includes Docker Engine, Docker CLI, and Docker Compose). You can download it from the official Docker website.
  • Basic terminal/command-line knowledge
⚠️ You can use any programming language or framework (like Python, Java, Go, .NET, etc.) — we’re just using Node.js with Express.js as a simple and popular example. The Dockerization process is largely the same. 🧰

Step 1: Create our Node.js Application

In this step, we’ll create a basic Express.js web server that responds with a simple message when accessed in the browser.. This minimal app will serve as our example to demonstrate how Docker packages and runs applications consistently across different environments.

First, create a new project folder and initialize a Node.js project:

mkdir docker-node-app && cd docker-node-appnpm init -y
  • mkdir docker-node-app: Creates a new folder for the project.
  • cd docker-node-app: Navigates into that folder.
  • npm init -y: Initializes a new Node.js project with default settings and generates a package.json file.
  • npm install express: Install ExpressExpresshelps you build web applications and APIs more easily than using plain Node.js).

Now, create a file named index.js inside the folder with the following content:

// index.jsconst express = require("express");const port = 3000;
app.get("/", (req, res) => res.send("Hello Docker!"));
  console.log(`Server running on http://localhost:${port}`)

This is a basic Express.js web server that:

  • Responds with “Hello Docker!”

You can test this locally by running node index.js and navigating to http://localhost:3000 in your browser.

📦 Ensure your package.json contains a start script so Docker knows how to run your app:

{  "name": "docker-node-app",  "main": "index.js",    "start": "node index.js"  "keywords": [],  "license": "ISC",
  "description": "A simple Node.js app to be Dockerized",
  "dependencies": {  }

Step 2: Create a Dockerfile

In the docker-node-app directory, create a file named Dockerfile (no extension):

# Use an official Node.js runtime as the base imageFROM node:22-alpine
# Set the working directory in the container
WORKDIR /app
# Copy package.json and package-lock.json to the working directory
COPY package*.json ./
# Install application dependencies
RUN npm install
# Copy the rest of the application code
COPY . .
# Expose the port the app runs on
EXPOSE 3000
# Define the command to run the application
CMD [ "npm", "start" ]

Let’s break down this Dockerfile:

  • FROM node:22-alpine: We start with a base image that includes Node.js (version 22, using the lightweight Alpine Linux distribution).
  • WORKDIR /app: Sets the working directory inside the container to /app.
  • COPY package*.json ./: Copies our package.json and package-lock.json files.
  • RUN npm install: Installs our Node.js dependencies.
  • COPY . .: Copies all remaining files from our current directory (your app code) into the container's /app directory (working directory).
  • EXPOSE 3000: Informs Docker that the container will listen on port 3000 at runtime.
  • CMD ["npm", "start"]: Defines the command to run our application when the container starts.

Step 3: Build the Docker Image

💡 : Make sure Docker is installed and on your system before executing the following commands. If you’re using Docker Desktop (Windows/macOS), ensure the app is open and Docker is active.

Open your terminal, navigate to the docker-node-app directory, and run the following command:

docker build -t docker-node-app .
  • docker build: The command to build a Docker image.
  • t docker-node-app: Tags (names) our image docker-node-app. You can choose any name.
  • .: Specifies the build context, which is the current directory (where your Dockerfile is located).

Docker will now execute the instructions in your Dockerfile, download the base image, install dependencies, and create your custom image. This might take a few minutes the first time.

You can verify the image was created by running:

You should see output similar to:

REPOSITORY          TAG       IMAGE ID       CREATED          SIZEdocker-node-app     latest    123abc456def   10 seconds ago   164MB

Step 4: Run the Docker Container

Now that we have our image, let’s run it as a container:

docker run -p 4000:3000 docker-node-app
  • docker run: The command to run a Docker container.
  • p 4000:3000: This is the crucial port mapping. It maps port 4000 on your host machine to port 3000 inside the container. So, when you access localhost:4000, it will forward the request to your Node.js app running on port 3000 inside the container.
  • docker-node-app: The name of the image we want to run.

You should see output similar to: Server running on http://localhost:3000 .

Step 5: Access Your Dockerized App

Open your web browser and navigate to http://localhost:4000. You should see the message: "Hello Docker!"

Congratulations 🎉! You’ve successfully Dockerized and run your first Node.js application!

🔍 Want to check running containers?

Open a new terminal tab/window and run:

This shows a list of currently running containers. You’ll see output like:

CONTAINER ID   IMAGE             COMMAND          STATUS         PORTS                    NAMESd2548908d52d   docker-node-app   "npm start"      Up 10 seconds  0.0.0.0:4000->3000/tcp   awesome_rubin

🛑 To stop the running container:

Copy the (e.g., d2548908d52d) and run:

That’s it! You’ve built, run, listed, and stopped your first Docker container. 🐳

💡 : You can upload this docker image to Docker Hub and pull it in other environments like staging or production. In those environments, you don’t need to install Node.js — Docker provides everything your app needs inside the container. That’s the beauty of containerization: consistent environments, no manual setup. 🚀
  • Understood why containers matter
  • Seen how containers differ from virtual machines
  • Explored Docker’s main tools
  • Understood Docker Workflow
  • Dockerized your first Node.js app!

This is just the tip of the iceberg! Docker opens up a world of possibilities for modern application development and deployment. Here are some next steps:

  • For multi-container applications (e.g., a Node.js app with a database), Docker Compose makes it incredibly easy to define and run them.
  • Learn about Docker Volumes: For persistent data storage (e.g., database data), volumes are essential.
  • Dive into Docker Networking: Understand how containers communicate with each other.
  • Push your image to Docker Hub: Share your Dockerized application with the world!
  • Discover container orchestration: For production deployments, look into Kubernetes or Docker Swarm for managing and scaling your containers.

items.title: Kubernetes & OpenShift: The Complete Platform Engineering Guide for Modern Container Orchestration
items.author: Salwan Mohamed
items.content:
From Basic Containers to Enterprise-Grade Orchestration Platforms

A comprehensive deep-dive into container orchestration evolution, architectural patterns, and production deployment strategies

Introduction: The Container Revolution and Beyond

The journey from traditional application deployment to modern container orchestration represents one of the most significant shifts in platform engineering. What started as a solution to the “works on my machine” problem has evolved into sophisticated orchestration platforms that power the world’s largest distributed systems.

In this comprehensive guide, we’ll explore the complete spectrum of container orchestration — from fundamental container concepts through Kubernetes architecture to enterprise-grade OpenShift platforms. Whether you’re a platform engineer designing infrastructure, a DevOps practitioner implementing CI/CD pipelines, or a system architect planning cloud-native migrations, this article provides the technical depth and practical insights you need.

Part 1: Container Fundamentals — Beyond the Basics

Understanding Container Architecture at the Kernel Level

Containers aren’t just “lightweight VMs” — they’re a sophisticated abstraction built on Linux kernel features that provide process isolation while sharing the host kernel. This architectural distinction is crucial for platform engineers who need to understand resource allocation, security boundaries, and performance characteristics.

Core Container Technologies:

provide process isolation across six key areas:

  • Process isolation
  • Network interface isolation
  • Filesystem mount point isolation
  • Hostname and domain name isolation
  • User and group ID isolation
  • Inter-process communication isolation

manage resource allocation:

  • CPU time slicing and throttling
  • Memory limits and swap accounting
  • Disk I/O bandwidth controls
  • Network bandwidth management

The Open Container Initiative (OCI) and Portability

The OCI specifications ensure container portability across different runtime environments. Understanding these standards is essential for platform engineers working with multi-cloud or hybrid deployments:

Defines how container images are built and structured OCI Runtime Specification: Defines how containers are executed OCI Distribution Specification: Defines how container images are distributed

This standardization enables the “build once, run anywhere” promise that makes containers so powerful for modern application deployment.

Part 2: Kubernetes Architecture — The Orchestration Foundation

Kubernetes transforms individual servers into a unified cluster through sophisticated control plane components. Understanding these components is crucial for platform engineers responsible for cluster operations and troubleshooting.

API Server (kube-apiserver):

  • RESTful interface for all cluster operations
  • Authentication and authorization enforcement
  • Admission controller processing
  • etcd communication gateway
  • Distributed key-value store for cluster state
  • Consensus protocol (Raft) for data consistency
  • Backup and restore procedures critical for disaster recovery

Scheduler (kube-scheduler):

  • Pod placement decisions based on resource requirements
  • Affinity/anti-affinity rules processing
  • Custom scheduler extensibility

Controller Manager (kube-controller-manager):

  • Node lifecycle management
  • Endpoint slice management
  • Service account and token management
  • Garbage collection operations

The Declarative Configuration Model

Kubernetes’ declarative approach fundamentally changes how we think about infrastructure management. Instead of imperative commands, we define desired states and let controllers ensure reality matches our declarations.

Example: Deployment Resource

apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: nginx-deployment    app: nginx  replicas: 3    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:      - name: nginx        ports:        resources:            memory: "64Mi"          limits:            cpu: "500m"

This declarative model enables GitOps workflows where infrastructure changes are version-controlled and automatically applied.

Pod Networking and Service Discovery

Kubernetes networking follows a flat network model where every pod gets a unique IP address within the cluster. This simplifies application development while providing sophisticated traffic management capabilities.

  • Container Network Interface (CNI): Pluggable networking layer
  • Service load balancing and traffic routing
  • Automatic service discovery through CoreDNS
  • External traffic routing and SSL termination

Service Types for Different Use Cases:

  • Internal cluster communication
  • External access on specific node ports
  • Cloud provider load balancer integration
  • DNS CNAME aliases for external services

Part 3: Advanced Kubernetes Features for Production

Horizontal Pod Autoscaling and Cluster Autoscaling

Production Kubernetes deployments require dynamic scaling capabilities to handle varying workloads efficiently. The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) and Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) provide automated resource optimization.

HPA Configuration Example:

apiVersion: autoscaling/v2kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler  name: nginx-hpa  scaleTargetRef:    kind: Deployment  minReplicas: 2  metrics:    resource:      target:        averageUtilization: 70    resource:      target:        averageUtilization: 80

Advanced Scaling Strategies:

  • Using historical data and machine learning
  • Application-specific metrics (queue length, response time)
  • Combining CPU, memory, and custom metrics
  • Scaling velocity and stabilization windows

Resource Management and Quality of Service

Kubernetes provides sophisticated resource management through requests and limits, enabling efficient cluster utilization while preventing resource contention.

  • Requests equal limits for all containers
  • At least one container has requests < limits
  • No requests or limits specified

Understanding QoS classes is crucial for platform engineers designing cluster resource allocation strategies and troubleshooting performance issues.

Part 4: OpenShift — Enterprise Kubernetes Platform

OpenShift’s Enterprise Enhancements

Red Hat OpenShift Container Platform builds upon Kubernetes’ foundation with enterprise-focused enhancements that address real-world operational challenges. These additions transform Kubernetes from an orchestration engine into a complete application platform.

Developer Experience Enhancements:

automates the container build process:

# Create application from source codeoc new-app python:3.8~https://github.com/your-org/python-app.git# 1. Detects Python application
# 2. Pulls base Python 3.8 image
# 3. Builds application container
# 4. Deploys with appropriate resources

Integrated CI/CD with OpenShift Pipelines:

  • Tekton-based pipeline engine
  • Artifact management with integrated registry
  • Multi-stage deployment automation

Security Enhancements and RBAC

OpenShift implements security-first design principles with enhanced Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Security Context Constraints (SCCs).

Security Context Constraints provide fine-grained pod security control:

apiVersion: security.openshift.io/v1kind: SecurityContextConstraints  name: custom-scc
allowHostDirVolumePlugin: false
allowHostIPC: falseallowHostPID: false
allowPrivilegedContainer: false
allowedCapabilities:runAsUser:  uidRangeMin: 1000
  • Project-level isolation with automatic namespace creation
  • Service account automation
  • OAuth integration with enterprise identity providers
  • Audit logging and compliance reporting

Red Hat Enterprise Linux CoreOS (RHEL CoreOS)

RHEL CoreOS represents a paradigm shift toward immutable infrastructure, designed specifically for containerized workloads.

  • Base system files are read-only
  • Machine Config Operator manages updates
  • Minimal attack surface with only required packages
  • Declarative first-boot system configuration
apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1kind: MachineConfig  labels:
    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker
  name: 99-worker-custom  config:      version: 3.1.0      units:
      - name: custom-service.service
        enabled: true          [Unit]
          Description=Custom Service
          [Service]
          ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/custom-service
          [Install]
          WantedBy=multi-user.target

Part 5: OpenShift Editions and Deployment Strategies

Managed vs. Self-Managed Decision Framework

Choosing between managed and self-managed OpenShift deployments requires careful consideration of operational capabilities, cost structures, and control requirements.

Managed OpenShift Services:

Red Hat OpenShift Service on AWS (ROSA):

  • Jointly managed by Red Hat and AWS
  • Integrated with AWS services (IAM, VPC, EBS)
  • 99.95% SLA with automated updates
  • Pay-as-you-go pricing model

Azure Red Hat OpenShift (ARO):

  • Azure Active Directory integration
  • Azure Monitor and Log Analytics integration
  • Consumption-based billing

Red Hat OpenShift on IBM Cloud:

  • IBM Cloud Pak integration
  • Watson AI services integration
  • IBM Cloud Object Storage integration
  • Enterprise security compliance

Self-Managed Edition Comparison

OpenShift Kubernetes Engine:

  • Core Kubernetes with enterprise hardening
  • Red Hat Enterprise Linux CoreOS
  • Basic web console and CLI tools
  • Foundation for custom platform builds

OpenShift Container Platform:

  • Full developer and operator experience
  • Integrated monitoring with Prometheus/Grafana
  • Built-in image registry and build capabilities
  • Advanced networking with OVN-Kubernetes
  • Advanced Cluster Management for multi-cluster operations
  • Advanced Cluster Security for comprehensive security scanning
  • Quay enterprise registry with vulnerability scanning
  • Red Hat’s most comprehensive offering

Decision Matrix for Edition Selection:

Part 6: OpenShift Web Console and Operations

Multi-Perspective Interface Design

The OpenShift web console provides role-specific interfaces that streamline common workflows for different user personas.

Administrator Perspective:

  • Cluster-level resource management
  • Node and machine configuration
  • Storage and networking administration
  • Security policy management
  • Monitoring and alerting configuration
  • Application-centric workflow
  • Topology view for service relationships
  • Integrated build and deployment tools
  • Environment-specific configurations
  • Application health monitoring

Key Operational Features:

Direct shell access to nodes and pods enables real-time troubleshooting:

# Access node through debug podoc debug node/worker01.example.comchroot /hostsystemctl status kubelet crio

Resource Quota Management:

apiVersion: v1kind: ResourceQuota  name: project-quota  hard:    requests.memory: 8Gi    limits.memory: 16Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "10"
    count/pods: "20"

Integrated Monitoring and Observability

OpenShift includes a complete observability stack that provides insights into both cluster and application performance.

  • Automatic metrics collection from cluster components
  • ServiceMonitor and PodMonitor custom resources
  • PromQL query interface for custom dashboards
  • Integration with Grafana for visualization
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1kind: PrometheusRule  name: custom-alerts  groups:    rules:
    - alert: HighPodMemoryUsage
      expr: |
        container_memory_usage_bytes{container!="POD",container!=""} / 
        container_spec_memory_limit_bytes > 0.8      labels:      annotations:
        summary: "Pod {{ $labels.pod }} memory usage is above 80%"
        description: "Pod {{ $labels.pod }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} has been using more than 80% of its memory limit for more than 5 minutes."

Log Aggregation with OpenShift Logging:

  • Elasticsearch, Fluentd, and Kibana (EFK) stack
  • Automatic log collection from all cluster components
  • Application log forwarding and retention policies
  • Security event log correlation

Part 7: Advanced OpenShift Patterns and Best Practices

GitOps with OpenShift GitOps (Argo CD)

OpenShift GitOps provides declarative, Git-driven deployment workflows that enable reliable, auditable application delivery.

GitOps Architecture Pattern:

apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1kind: Application  name: sample-app
  namespace: openshift-gitops
spec:  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/your-org/app-config.git
    path: overlays/production  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: production    automated:      selfHeal: true    - CreateNamespace=true

Benefits of GitOps Implementation:

  • Entire system state described in Git
  • Every change is tracked and auditable
  • Deployments are reproducible and consistent
  • Changes are pulled rather than pushed to clusters

Multi-Cluster Management with Advanced Cluster Management

For enterprise deployments, managing multiple OpenShift clusters across different environments and cloud providers requires sophisticated orchestration capabilities.

Advanced Cluster Management Features:

  • Cluster Lifecycle Management: Automated cluster provisioning and updates
  • Application Lifecycle Management: Multi-cluster application deployment
  • Governance, Risk, and Compliance: Policy-driven cluster configuration
  • Centralized monitoring across cluster fleet

Multi-Cluster Application Deployment:

apiVersion: apps.open-cluster-management.io/v1kind: Subscription  name: multi-cluster-app  channel: dev-channel/sample-app    placementRef:
      name: production-clusters
  packageOverrides:
  - packageName: sample-app
    packageAlias: prod-app    - path: spec.replicas

Service Mesh Integration with OpenShift Service Mesh

OpenShift Service Mesh (based on Istio) provides advanced traffic management, security, and observability for microservices architectures.

Service Mesh Control Plane:

apiVersion: maistra.io/v2kind: ServiceMeshControlPlane  name: production  version: v2.4    type: Jaeger    grafana:    kiali:    prometheus:  security:      mtls: true

Traffic Management Capabilities:

  • Automatic failure isolation
  • Configurable retry mechanisms
  • Canary and blue-green deployments
  • Chaos engineering capabilities

Part 8: Production Deployment Strategies and Day-2 Operations

Cluster Sizing and Resource Planning

Proper cluster sizing is crucial for both performance and cost optimization. Platform engineers must balance resource utilization with headroom for scaling and maintenance operations.

Node Sizing Considerations:

  • Reserve 1 core per node for system processes
  • Reserve 1GB + 10% of total memory for system processes
  • Separate etcd storage from container workload storage
  • Consider east-west traffic patterns and bandwidth requirements

Example Production Cluster Architecture:

Control Plane Nodes (3):- 4 vCPU, 16GB RAM, 100GB SSD
- Dedicated for cluster management
- No workload scheduling- 8 vCPU, 32GB RAM, 200GB SSD
- General workload scheduling
- Horizontal scaling capability
Infrastructure Nodes (3):
- 4 vCPU, 16GB RAM, 500GB SSD
- Monitoring, logging, registry
- Separate billing/resource tracking

Backup and Disaster Recovery

Enterprise OpenShift deployments require comprehensive backup and disaster recovery strategies that address both cluster state and application data.

# Automated etcd backup scriptoc get no -l node-role.kubernetes.io/master -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}'
oc debug node/$NODE_NAME -- chroot /host /usr/local/bin/cluster-backup.sh /home/core/backup

Application Data Backup with OADP:

apiVersion: velero.io/v1kind: Backup  name: application-backup  includedNamespaces:  storageLocation: default  - default

Disaster Recovery Testing:

  • Regular restore testing in isolated environments
  • Recovery Time Objective (RTO) and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) validation
  • Cross-region backup replication for geographic redundancy
  • Automated failover procedures with documented runbooks

Security Hardening and Compliance

Production OpenShift clusters require comprehensive security hardening that addresses multiple threat vectors and compliance requirements.

Network Security Policies:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1kind: NetworkPolicy  name: deny-all-ingress  podSelector: {}  - IngressapiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
spec:    matchLabels:  policyTypes:  ingress:    - podSelector:          app: frontend    - protocol: TCP
apiVersion: v1kind: Namespace  name: secure-namespace    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted

Compliance Automation with Compliance Operator:

  • Automated security scanning with OpenSCAP
  • CIS Kubernetes Benchmark implementation
  • NIST 800–190 container security guidelines
  • Continuous compliance monitoring and reporting

Conclusion: The Future of Container Orchestration

The evolution from basic containerization to sophisticated orchestration platforms like OpenShift represents a fundamental shift in how we design, deploy, and operate distributed systems. As platform engineers, understanding this progression — from container internals through Kubernetes architecture to enterprise platforms — enables us to make informed decisions about technology adoption and implementation strategies.

Key Takeaways for Platform Engineers:

Deep understanding of container fundamentals, Kubernetes architecture, and OpenShift enhancements enables better troubleshooting and optimization decisions.

Implementing proper monitoring, backup strategies, and security hardening ensures reliable production operations.

Choosing between managed and self-managed deployments, selecting appropriate editions, and planning for scale requires balancing technical capabilities with business requirements.

Understanding trends like GitOps, service mesh, and multi-cluster management positions teams for evolving architectural patterns.

Emerging Trends and Technologies:

WebAssembly (WASM) Integration: Container runtimes are beginning to support WASM workloads, potentially changing performance and security characteristics.

Lightweight Kubernetes distributions and edge-optimized deployments are expanding orchestration to resource-constrained environments.

AI/ML Workload Optimization: Specialized operators and resource scheduling for machine learning workloads are becoming standard platform features.

Sustainability and Green Computing: Resource optimization and carbon-aware scheduling are emerging as important platform engineering considerations.

Getting Started: Practical Next Steps

  1. Set up OpenShift Local for hands-on experimentation
  2. Complete Red Hat’s DO180 course for structured learning
  3. Practice with the Developer Sandbox for real cluster experience
  4. Contribute to open-source Kubernetes projects
  1. Conduct proof-of-concept deployments with representative workloads
  2. Develop migration strategies for existing applications
  3. Establish monitoring and alerting baselines
  4. Create disaster recovery and testing procedures

For Organizational Strategy:

  1. Assess current infrastructure and application portfolios
  2. Define cloud-native transformation roadmaps
  3. Establish center of excellence for container platforms
  4. Plan for skills development and training programs

The container orchestration landscape continues to evolve rapidly, but the fundamental principles of declarative management, automated operations, and platform thinking will remain central to successful implementations. By mastering these technologies and patterns, platform engineers can build robust, scalable, and efficient infrastructure that enables business innovation and growth.

Continue your platform engineering journey by exploring advanced topics like multi-cluster federation, progressive delivery patterns, and cloud-native security strategies. The intersection of containers, orchestration, and enterprise platforms represents one of the most dynamic areas in modern infrastructure technology.

items.author: Celestine Ntadi
items.content:
From Zero to VibeRoll: Building a Production-Ready DevOps Stack on AWS with Terraform & GitHub Actions

An educational deep dive into deploying a scalable, secure cloud application using DevOps best practices.

In this post, I’ll walk you through how I built A modern backend service using a fully automated, production-grade cloud infrastructure powered by:

  • : ECS Fargate, RDS PostgreSQL, ElastiCache Redis, ALB, ECR, Secrets Manager
  • : Modular infrastructure as code
  • : End-to-end CI/CD automation
  • : Validated, versioned, and secure

Whether you’re a cloud engineer, DevOps enthusiast, or backend developer, this post will guide you through setting up your own secure and scalable infrastructure.

is a stateless Node.js API deployed on AWS using container orchestration, persistent storage, a caching layer, and secrets-driven CI/CD pipelines.

  • Node.js API on
  • Secure secrets in
  • Infrastructure managed with
  • VPC with private/public subnets, NAT Gateway, and 
  • provides serverless container orchestration, reducing ops overhead.
  • is a secure and scalable container image registry tightly integrated with ECS.
  • manages our relational data layer, providing automated backups and failover.
  • boosts application performance with low-latency caching.
  • ensures traffic is distributed intelligently with built-in health checks.
  • centralizes and encrypts environment secrets for applications.
  • handles continuous integration and deployment from the repo to the cloud.
  • provides comprehensive observability across the full stack, encompassing logs, metrics, and alarms.
  • create a secure and flexible network layout with controlled internet access.
  • protect against common web exploits and control traffic at the instance level.
  • in public subnet routes traffic to tasks in private subnets
  • stores Docker images securely
  • and are private and protected
  • GitHub Actions automates infrastructure provisioning and app deployment.
  • NAT Gateway allows secure outbound-only internet access
  • for ECS, RDS, Redis
  • for secure internet access from private subnets
module "vpc" {  source   = "./modules/vpc"  azs      = var.azs

To keep the infrastructure maintainable, the project uses a modular layout:

celestn@CN001:/viberoll-project/viberoll-infra$ tree -I 'node_modules' -L 2.├── backend.tf│   └── viberoll_architecture_improved.png├── modules│   ├── cloudwatch│   ├── ecs│   ├── rds│   ├── vpc├── outputs.tf└── variables.tf
resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {  identifier = "${var.project_name}-postgres"  db_name    = var.db_name
  username   = var.db_username
  password   = var.db_password

Secrets Management with AWS

Secrets are passed via GitHub Secrets and provisioned in AWS Secrets Manager using Terraform:

resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret" "secrets" {  for_each = var.secrets_map
  name     = "${var.project_name}-${each.key}"
}
resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "secrets_version" {
  for_each        = aws_secretsmanager_secret.secrets
  secret_id       = each.value.id
  secret_string   = var.secrets_map[each.key]
  version_stages  = ["AWSCURRENT"]
}

✅ CI fails fast if secrets are missing✅ Secrets validated before terraform apply

CI/CD with GitHub Actions

The CI/CD workflow handles validation, planning, and deployment:

- name: Terraform Plan  run: |
    terraform plan -input=false -out=tfplan \
      -var-file="secrets.auto.tfvars.json" \
      -var="project_name=${TF_PROJECT_NAME}" \
      -var="db_username=${TF_DB_USERNAME}"
  • Terraform init, plan, apply
  • Fail early on secret misconfigurations
  • Use the ALB DNS output to test the endpoint in your browser or curl.

Allow your IP temporarily in the security group and test with:

psql -h <endpoint> -U viberoll_service -d viberoll -p 5432
  • Secrets missing AWSCURRENT label
  • Terraform for_each failing on dynamic keys.
  • Cost challenges in multi-AZ during dev
  • Forced AWSCURRENT version stage
  • Null resource to validate secrets
  • Used single-AZ RDS for development

This project proves that building secure, production-ready cloud-native applications is achievable with the right tools and practices:

  • ✅ Security-first infrastructure (VPC, IAM, WAF)
  • ✅ Modular, reusable IaC (Terraform)
  • ✅ Fully automated CI/CD (GitHub Actions)
  • ✅ Testing is baked into every step

Application Load Balancer

Elastic Container Registry

Fork the repo, plug in your values, and you’ve got yourself a fully deployable, cloud-native backend, ready for production.

Have questions or improvements? Drop a comment, let’s connect!
